title: Apply: potential energy
description: Apply your knowledge of potential energy in this set of free practice questions.

assessment_items.question_content:
A boy holds a ball at shoulder level before throwing it.  Four possible paths the ball could travel and the locations of the ball at some point along those paths are shown below.

**For each location, select whether the potential energy stored in the ball-Earth system is more, less, or the same as when the ball was held at the boy’s shoulder.**

[[☃ label-image 1]]
hints: The ball and Earth interact gravitationally. A gravitational interaction is always *attractive.*
If two objects that attract each other are moved farther apart, the potential energy stored between them increases.  If they are moved closer together, the potential energy decreases.

So when the ball is at the same reference height it started at, the ball-Earth system has the same amount of gravitational potential energy it started with.

When the ball is *higher* than the reference height it started at, the ball-Earth system has *more* gravitational potential energy than it started with.

When the ball is *lower* than the reference height it started at, the ball-Earth system has *less* gravitational potential energy than it started with.

The answers are:
[[☃ image 1]]

options.imageAlt: Two boys stand facing each other, each on top of a hill with a small valley between. The boy on the left holds a ball at his shoulder. A dotted horizontal line runs through this ball and across the image.

markers.label: The ball is in the air, on the same horizontal line that runs through the initial position of the ball, but to the right of the boy. The path is a tall arc from the boy to this point.
The ball is in a tree on top of the hill at the right side of the image. It is higher than the initial position of the ball. The path is an arc from the boy to the tree.
The ball is in the hand of the boy on the right. It is at the same height as the initial position of the ball. Its path is a low arc between the two boys.
The ball is rolling down and to the right, along the left hill. The path is a short arc from the boy to the ground in front of him, and then follows the surface of the hill.

assessment_items.question_content:
A boy holds a ball at shoulder level before throwing it.  Four possible paths the ball could travel and the locations of the ball at some point along those paths are shown below.

**For each location, select whether the potential energy stored in the ball-Earth system is more, less, or the same as when the ball was held at the boy’s shoulder.**

[[☃ label-image 1]]
hints: The ball and Earth interact gravitationally. A gravitational interaction is always *attractive.*
If two objects that attract each other are moved farther apart, the potential energy stored between them increases.  If they are moved closer together, the potential energy decreases.

So when the ball is at the same reference height it started at, the ball-Earth system has the same amount of gravitational potential energy it started with.

When the ball is *higher* than the reference height it started at, the ball-Earth system has *more* gravitational potential energy than it started with.

When the ball is *lower* than the reference height it started at, the ball-Earth system has *less* gravitational potential energy than it started with.

The answers are:
[[☃ image 2]]

options.imageAlt: Two boys stand facing each other, each on top of a hill with a small valley between. A tree is on the far right of the image. The boy on the left holds a ball at his shoulder. A dotted horizontal line runs through this ball and across the image.

markers.label: The ball moved down and to the right, bounced off the ground and is in the air, moving to the right and slightly upward. It is below the horizontal line passing through the initial position of the ball.
The ball is in the hand of the boy on the right. It is slightly lower than the initial position of the ball. Its path is a low arc between the two boys.
The ball is in the tree on the right side of the image. It is on the horizontal line that passes through the ball’s initial position. The path is an arc from the boy to the tree.
The ball is in the air, following the path of a tall arc from the boy to this point. It is above the horizontal line that passes through the ball’s initial position.

assessment_items.question_content:
A boy holds a ball at shoulder level before throwing it.  Four possible paths the ball could travel and the locations of the ball at some point along those paths are shown below.

**For each location, select whether the potential energy stored in the ball-Earth system is more, less, or the same as when the ball was held at the boy’s shoulder.**

[[☃ label-image 1]]
hints: The ball and Earth interact gravitationally. A gravitational interaction is always *attractive.*
If two objects that attract each other are moved farther apart, the potential energy stored between them increases.  If they are moved closer together, the potential energy decreases.

So when the ball is at the same reference height it started at, the ball-Earth system has the same amount of gravitational potential energy it started with.

When the ball is *higher* than the reference height it started at, the ball-Earth system has *more* gravitational potential energy than it started with.

When the ball is *lower* than the reference height it started at, the ball-Earth system has *less* gravitational potential energy than it started with.

The answers are:

[[☃ image 4]]

options.imageAlt: Two boys stand facing each other, each on top of a hill with a small valley between. The boy on the left holds a ball at his shoulder. The boy on the right stands on a rock under a tree. A dotted horizontal line runs through this ball and across the image.

markers.label: The ball is in the air, following the path of a tall arc from the boy to this point. It is on the horizontal line that passes through the ball’s initial position.

The ball moved down and to the right, bounced off the ground and is in the air, moving to the right and slightly upward. It is below the horizontal line passing through the initial position of the ball.
The ball is in the hand of the boy on the right. It is slightly above the horizontal line that passes through the ball’s initial position. Its path is a low arc between the two boys.

The ball travelled in an arc from the boy to the tree, bounced off two of the tree branches, then landed on the ground. It is below the horizontal line that passes through the ball’s initial position.

assessment_items.question_content:
A red and a blue balloon are held at rest near each other on a tabletop. Both balloons were rubbed with a cloth to give them an electric charge. A top view of the balloons is shown below.

[[☃ image 2]]

The red balloon is released and moves away from the blue balloon. The blue balloon continues to be held at rest.

**Complete the statements for the time just after the red balloon is released.** 

* The blue balloon [[☃ dropdown 1]] kinetic energy.

* The red balloon [[☃ dropdown 2]] kinetic energy.

* The two-balloon system [[☃ dropdown 3]] electric potential energy.
hints:
The two balloons are initially at rest with zero kinetic energy. The blue balloon remains at rest. So, it *neither gains nor loses* kinetic energy.

When the red balloon is released it starts to move. So, it *gains* kinetic energy.

The red balloon moves away when released. So, we know it’s being repelled by the blue balloon.

Since the balloons interact over a distance, we know the two-balloon system initially contains electric potential energy.

As the red balloon moves away it gains kinetic energy. This is caused by a repelling force from the blue balloon. So, the kinetic energy gained by the red balloon *comes from* its interaction with the blue balloon. 

This means some of the potential energy that was initially stored *between* the two balloons must be transferred to the red balloon alone, in the form of kinetic energy. Therefore, the two-balloon system *loses* potential energy.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The answers are:

* The blue balloon neither gains nor loses kinetic energy.
* The red balloon gains kinetic energy.
* The two-balloon system loses electric potential energy.

options.alt: A red balloon on the left and a blue balloon on the right both sit on top of a surface. Each balloon has a hand on top of it.

assessment_items.question_content:
A red and a blue balloon are held at rest near each other on a tabletop. Both balloons were rubbed with a cloth to give them an electric charge. A top view of the balloons is shown below.

[[☃ image 2]]

The blue balloon is released and moves away from the red balloon. The red balloon continues to be held at rest.

**Complete the statements for the time just after the blue balloon is released.** 

*  The two-balloon system [[☃ dropdown 4]] electric potential energy.

* The blue balloon [[☃ dropdown 1]] kinetic energy.

* The red balloon [[☃ dropdown 2]] kinetic energy.
hints:
The two balloons are initially at rest with zero kinetic energy. When the blue balloon is released it starts to move. So, it *gains* kinetic energy.

The red balloon remains at rest. So, it *neither gains nor loses* kinetic energy.

The blue balloon moves away when released. So, we know it’s being repelled by the red balloon.

Since the balloons interact over a distance, we know the two-balloon system initially contains electric potential energy.

As the blue balloon moves away it gains kinetic energy. This is caused by a repelling force from the red balloon. So, the kinetic energy gained by the blue balloon *comes from* its interaction with the red balloon. 

This means some of the potential energy that was initially stored *between* the two balloons must be transferred to the blue balloon alone, in the form of kinetic energy. Therefore, the two-balloon system *loses* potential energy.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The answers are:

* The two-balloon system loses electric potential energy.
* The blue balloon gains kinetic energy.
* The red balloon neither gains nor loses kinetic energy.

options.alt: A red balloon on the left and a blue balloon on the right both sit on top of a surface. Each balloon has a hand on top of it.

assessment_items.question_content:
A red and a blue balloon are held at rest near each other on a tabletop. The red balloon was rubbed with a cloth and the blue balloon was rubbed with a plastic bag. This gave each of them an electric charge. A top view of the balloons is shown below.

[[☃ image 2]]

The red balloon is released and moves towards the blue balloon. The blue balloon continues to be held at rest.

**Complete the statements for the time just after the red balloon is released.** 

* The red balloon [[☃ dropdown 1]] kinetic energy.

* The blue balloon [[☃ dropdown 2]] kinetic energy.

* The two-balloon system [[☃ dropdown 3]] electric potential energy.
hints:
The two balloons are initially at rest with zero kinetic energy. When the red balloon is released it starts to move. So, it *gains* kinetic energy.

The blue balloon remains at rest. So, it *neither gains nor loses* kinetic energy.

The red balloon moves towards the blue balloon when released. So, we know it’s being attracted by the blue balloon.

Since the balloons interact over a distance, we know the two-balloon system initially contains electric potential energy.

As the red balloon moves towards the blue balloon it gains kinetic energy. This is caused by an attractive force from the blue balloon. So, the kinetic energy gained by the red balloon *comes from* its interaction with the blue balloon. 

This means some of the potential energy that was initially stored *between* the two balloons must be transferred to the red balloon alone, in the form of kinetic energy. Therefore, the two-balloon system *loses* potential energy.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The answers are:

* The red balloon gains kinetic energy.
* The blue balloon neither gains nor loses kinetic energy.
* The two-balloon system loses electric potential energy.

options.alt: A red balloon on the left and a blue balloon on the right both sit on top of a surface. Each balloon has a hand on top of it.

assessment_items.question_content:
Two different systems are shown below. Each system is shown before and after its arrangement changes.

[[☃ image 32]]
[[☃ image 33]]

**How did the potential energy of each system change?**

*  The magnetic potential energy of the two-magnet system [[☃ dropdown 13]].

* The electric potential energy of the balloon-hair system [[☃ dropdown 14]].
hints: Potential energy is stored between two objects that interact through a field. The objects can either attract or repel each other. When the arrangement of objects in the system changes, the potential energy stored between those objects changes.

The two magnets with their north poles facing each other *repel*. The photos show them moving *closer together*. When interacting objects move opposite the direction of the forces they exert on each other, the potential energy stored between them increases.

The charged balloon and hair *attract*. The photos show them moving *closer together*. When interacting objects move in the direction of the forces they exert on each other, the potential energy stored between them decreases.

The answers are:

* The magnetic potential energy of the two-magnet system increased.

* The electric potential energy of the balloon-hair system decreased.

options.alt:
Two images of a pair of bar magnets. 

The top image is labeled "before." The two magnets are held so the ends marked "N" are nearest each other and the ends marked "S" are furthest from each other.

The bottom image is labeled "after." The two magnets are in the same orientation, but the two ends marked "N" are closer together.
options.caption: *Two-magnet system (north poles facing each other)*

options.alt:
On the left is an image, labeled "before," of a young girl whose hair is sticking out to the left toward a balloon held to the left of her head. 

On the right is an image, labeled "after," of the same girl with hair sticking out toward the balloon. The balloon is still to the left of her head but is closer than before.
options.caption: *Charged balloon-hair system*

assessment_items.question_content:
Two different systems are shown below. Each system is shown before and after its arrangement changes.

[[☃ image 35]]
[[☃ image 34]]

**How did the potential energy of each system change?**

*  The magnetic potential energy of the two-magnet system [[☃ dropdown 13]].

* The gravitational potential energy of the dog-Earth system [[☃ dropdown 14]].
hints: Potential energy is stored between two objects that interact through a field. The objects can either attract or repel each other. When the arrangement of objects in the system changes, the potential energy stored between those objects changes.

The two magnets with their north poles facing each other *repel*. The photos show them moving *farther apart*. When interacting objects move in the direction of the forces they exert on each other, the potential energy stored between them decreases.

The dog and Earth *attract*. The photos show them moving *farther apart*. When interacting objects move opposite the direction of the forces they exert on each other, the potential energy stored between them increases.

The answers are:

* The magnetic potential energy of the two-magnet system decreased.

* The gravitational potential energy of the dog-Earth system increased.

options.alt:
On the left is an image, labeled "before," of a dog approaching an obstacle that consists of horizontal bars the dog must jump over.

On the right is an image labeled, "after," of the dog in the air, above the bars of the obstacle.
options.caption: *Dog-Earth system*

options.alt:
Two images of a pair of bar magnets. 

The top image is labeled "before." The two magnets are held so the ends marked "N" are nearest each other and the ends marked "S" are furthest from each other.

The bottom image is labeled "after." The two magnets are in the same orientation, but the two ends marked "N" are farther apart.
options.caption: *Two-magnet system (north poles facing each other)*

assessment_items.question_content:
Two different systems are shown below. Each system is shown before and after its arrangement changes.

[[☃ image 34]]
[[☃ image 36]]

**How did the potential energy of each system change?**

*  The gravitational potential energy of the dog-Earth system [[☃ dropdown 13]].

* The electric potential energy of the charged balloon-hair system [[☃ dropdown 14]].
hints: Potential energy is stored between two objects that interact through a field. The objects can either attract or repel each other. When the arrangement of objects in the system changes, the potential energy stored between those objects changes.

The dog and Earth *attract.* The photos show them moving *closer together.* When interacting objects move in the direction of the forces they exert on each other, the potential energy stored between them decreases.

The charged balloon and hair *attract*. The photos show them moving *farther apart*. When interacting objects move opposite the direction of the forces they exert on each other, the potential energy stored between them increases.

The answers are:

* The gravitational potential energy of the dog-Earth system decreased.

* The gravitational potential energy of the balloon-hair system increased.

options.alt:
On the left is an image, labeled "before," of a dog in the air, above an obstacle that consists of horizontal bars the dog must jump over.

On the right is an image labeled, "after," of the dog landing on the ground in front of the obstacle.
options.caption: *Dog-Earth system*

options.alt:
On the left is an image, labeled "before," of a young girl whose hair is sticking to a balloon a short distance to the left of her head. 

On the right is an image, labeled "after," of the same girl with the balloon a farther distance to the left of her head. Her hair is sticking out toward the balloon.
options.caption: *Charged balloon-hair system*

assessment_items.question_content:
A magnet is at rest on the side of a refrigerator. The magnet experiences forces due to interactions with other objects, as modeled below.

 [[☃ image 1]]

**Which of these interactions store potential energy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A magnet sticks to the left edge of a refrigerator. Four labeled arrows point outward from the magnet. One arrow, labeled F friction, points upward. An equal-length arrow, labeled F gravity, points downward.  A third arrow, labeled F normal, points leftward. An equal-length arrow, labeled F magnetic, points rightward.

choices.content: the magnetic interaction between the magnet and the refrigerator
the gravitational interaction between the magnet and the Earth
the frictional interaction between the magnet and the refrigerator
the normal interaction between the magnet and the refrigerator

assessment_items.question_content:
A magnet and nail on a tabletop attract each other. The nail slides toward the magnet while the magnet stays at rest. The nail experiences forces due to interactions with other objects, as modeled below.

 [[☃ image 5]]

**Which of these interactions store potential energy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A bar magnet rests on a flat surface. To the right of the magnet, a nail is on the same surface. Four labeled arrows point outward from the nail. One arrow, labeled F normal, points upward. An equal-length arrow, labeled F gravity, points downward.  A third arrow, labeled F magnetic, points leftward. A shorter arrow, labeled F friction, points rightward.

choices.content: the magnetic interaction between the nail and the magnet
the gravitational interaction between the nail and the Earth
the frictional interaction between the nail and the table
the normal interaction between the nail and the table

assessment_items.question_content:
A balloon is given an electric charge by rubbing it on a sweater. Then it's placed on a wall, and it sticks to the wall. The balloon experiences forces due to interactions with other objects, as modeled below.

[[☃ image 5]]

**Which of these interactions store potential energy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A balloon is touching a vertical surface to its right.  Four labeled arrows point outward from the balloon. One arrow, labeled F friction, points upward. An equal-length arrow, labeled F gravity, points downward.  A third arrow, labeled F normal, points leftward. An equal-length arrow, labeled F electric, points rightward.

choices.content: the electric interaction between the balloon and the wall
the gravitational interaction between the balloon and the Earth
the frictional interaction between the balloon and the wall
the normal interaction between the balloon and the wall
